---
title: "How We Made This"
page-layout: article
toc: true
toc-location: left
title-block-banner: true
header-includes:
  - '<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.0.0-beta3/css/all.min.css">'
  - '<link href="https://unpkg.com/tabulator-tables@6.3.0/dist/css/tabulator.min.css" rel="stylesheet">'
  - '<script src="https://unpkg.com/tabulator-tables@6.3.0/dist/js/tabulator.min.js"></script>'
  - '<script type="text/javascript" src="https://oss.sheetjs.com/sheetjs/xlsx.full.min.js"></script>'
---

## Quarto

This website is built with [Quarto](https://quarto.org/), an open-source scientific and technical publishing system. Quarto supports Python, R, and JavaScript (in the form of Observable notebooks), making it possible to combine narrative text, code, and interactive elements in a single, reproducible workflow. Quarto can also render to multiple output formats, including PDFs and Word documents.

## Observable Notebooks & the Post45 Data Viewer

The interactive data tables on this site are powered by [Tabulator](http://tabulator.info/) and [Observable](https://observablehq.com/) notebooks, built on top of the [Post45 Data Viewer](https://observablehq.com/d/8bb63a6cde9addff). The Post45 Data Viewer was developed by Melanie Walsh and Juan Carlos Garcia with support from the [Post45 Data Collective](https://data.post45.org/) and [Mozilla's Responsible Computing Challenge](https://foundation.mozilla.org/en/what-we-fund/awards/responsible-computing-challenge/).

These tables allow users to filter, sort, search, and download data; view tooltips with additional context; and customize which columns are displayed. Here's an example using the ["Time Horizons of Futuristic Fiction"](posts/futuristic-fiction/) dataset:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
import {generateTabulatorTableFromCSV} from "8bb63a6cde9addff"
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
generateTabulatorTableFromCSV(
  "#table-container-example",
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Post45-Data-Collective/data/refs/heads/main/futuristic_fiction/futuristic_fiction.csv",
  {
    displayedColumns: [
      "title",
      "creator",
      "year_set",
      "released",
      "years_distant",
      "medium",
      "genre"
    ],
    columnPopups: [
      "Title of the work.",
      "Creator (author, director, etc.) of the work.",
      "Future year in which the work takes place.",
      "Year the work was originally released.",
      "How far in the future the work is set (year_set minus released).",
      "Medium: fiction, film, television, video game, radio, comics, etc.",
      "Genre from Wikidata (e.g., science fiction, cyberpunk, crime, etc.)."
    ],
    columnWidths: { "medium": 130, "genre": 160 },
    rangeSliderColumns: ["year_set", "released"],
    numericColumns: ["years_distant"],
    categoryColumns: ["medium", "genre"],
    sortColumns: ["year_set"],
    sortOrders: ["desc"]
  }
);
```

::: {#table-container-example style="height: 500px"}
:::

### Use the Post45 Data Viewer with Your Own Data

You can integrate the Post45 Data Viewer into any Quarto page. For more detailed documentation, see the [Post45 Data Viewer Observable notebook](https://observablehq.com/d/8bb63a6cde9addff). To get started, load the required libraries and import the viewer, then call `generateTabulatorTableFromCSV()` with a URL or file path to your CSV and a configuration object.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Example Code"}

**1. Load the required libraries** by adding these lines to your `.qmd` file:

```html
<link href="https://unpkg.com/tabulator-tables@6.3.0/dist/css/tabulator.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
<script src="https://unpkg.com/tabulator-tables@6.3.0/dist/js/tabulator.min.js"></script>
<script src="https://oss.sheetjs.com/sheetjs/xlsx.full.min.js"></script>
```

**2. Import the Data Viewer** in an OJS code block. The string `"8bb63a6cde9addff"` is the unique ID of the [Post45 Data Viewer Observable notebook](https://observablehq.com/d/8bb63a6cde9addff) — this is how Observable identifies and imports shared notebooks:

````markdown
```{ojs}
//| echo: false
import {generateTabulatorTableFromCSV} from "8bb63a6cde9addff"
```
````

**3. Call the function** with your data URL and configuration:

````markdown
```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false
generateTabulatorTableFromCSV(
  "#my-table",                          // CSS selector for the table container
  "https://your-url.com/data.csv",      // <-- plug in your CSV URL or file path here

  {
    displayedColumns: [                 // columns to show (in order)
      "column_a",
      "column_b",
      "column_c"
    ],

    columnPopups: [                     // tooltip for each displayed column (same order)
      "Description of column A.",
      "Description of column B.",
      "Description of column C."
    ],

    numericColumns: ["column_c"],       // columns to treat as numbers (enables range filters)

    categoryColumns: ["column_a"],      // columns to treat as categories (enables dropdown filters)

    sortColumns: ["column_a"],          // default sort column(s)
    sortOrders: ["asc"]                 // "asc" or "desc" for each sort column
  }
);
```
````

**4. Add a container div** where you want the table to appear:

```markdown
::: {#my-table style="height: 500px"}
:::
```

The `#my-table` ID must match the first argument to `generateTabulatorTableFromCSV()`.

:::
